journal-title-group.journal-title: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews

publisher.publisher-name: John Wiley and Sons, Inc. and the Cochrane Library

title-group.article-title: Post‐exposure prophylaxis with antibiotics to prevent sexually transmitted infections

author-notes.corresp: Koko Shibutani, Division of Infectious Diseases, St Luke's International University, 9-1, Akashi-cho, Chuo-ku, Tokyo, 1048560, Japan. kokoshib@luke.ac.jp.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2026 The Authors. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. on behalf of The Cochrane Collaboration.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non-Commercial Licence , which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

license-p.italic: The content of this article (as distinct from the content of the ‘Supplementary materials’, ‘Related’ and ‘About this Review’ sections) has been peer reviewed and prepared for publication in accordance with

. Though the aim of these processes is, among other things, to check the accuracy of such content, responsibility for the content remains that of the author(s) and Cochrane gives no representations or warranties of any kind in relation to, and accepts no liability for any reliance on or use of, the content of the article.

abstract.title: Objectives
abstract.p: This is a protocol for a Cochrane Review (intervention). The objectives are as follows:

To assess the benefits and harms of taking antibiotics after sexual intercourse as post‐exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for the incidence of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in adolescents and adults.

sec.title: Description of the condition
sec.p: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are present worldwide, and their epidemiology varies depending on the availability of testing and treatment, as well as social and cultural determinants [1, 2]. STIs have been increasing globally and have become a major public health problem [3, 4]. Some anatomical factors, such as differences in mucosal epithelium (e.g. columnar versus squamous epithelium), susceptibility to microtrauma, and variations in mucosal surface area and local immune environment, lead to differences in STI risk and a higher likelihood of asymptomatic STIs [5]. People who are capable of pregnancy may be more likely to experience complications from untreated STIs, such as infertility or adverse pregnancy outcomes [1, 5]. Adolescents and cisgender women are at high risk of STIs, regardless of their sexual orientation or sexual behaviour [6, 7, 8]. The higher risk of STIs in these populations also translates into a significant disease burden among women who are or will become pregnant and their newborns. This includes reduced fertility due to STI‐related complications, such as damage to the fallopian tubes [9, 10], and the impact of congenital infections on neonates, such as congenital syphilis [11, 12].

STI incidence is highest among people who engage in high‐risk sexual behaviours, such as unprotected sex (i.e. sex without a condom) and having multiple sexual partners [13, 14, 15]. In addition, living in a community with fewer resources and lower access to health care can also be associated with higher STI incidence [16, 17]. Also, there is an increased risk of STIs among men who have sex with men (MSM) and transgender individuals [18, 19, 20]. Some people at increased risk for STIs, including MSM and transgender individuals, may experience stigma and reduced access to testing and treatment. The increased risk of STIs, combined with stigma and reduced access to testing and treatment among these populations, constitutes significant social and public health issues that warrant the implementation of effective STI prevention strategies.

Not everyone can undergo STI testing and treatment. Thus, preventive interventions are used to reduce STI incidence, such as pre‐exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV prevention [15, 21]. More common bacterial STIs that are treated with antibiotics can also be prevented with prophylactic antibiotic use [22, 23, 24]. The use of PrEP for preventing HIV among people at high risk of HIV infection has raised concerns about increased rates of STIs [15, 25]. There are concerns that the availability of PrEP for individuals at high risk of HIV and other STIs may lead to increased risk compensation behaviours, such as reduced condom use and an increased number of sexual partners, potentially contributing to an increase in STI incidence. A decrease in condom use has been observed across diverse populations, leading to concerns about a potential rise in the incidence of other STIs not covered by PrEP, especially among high‐risk groups [14, 26, 27].

sec.title: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs)
sec.p: STIs, such as syphilis, gonorrhoea, and chlamydia, are major public health problems in both resource‐rich and resource‐limited settings. Surveillance data from the USA suggest that approximately two‐thirds of incident chlamydia infections and one‐half of incident gonococcal infections occur in adolescents and young adults [28]. According to a report by the World Health Organization (WHO), over one million curable STIs are newly acquired each day among people aged 15 to 49 years worldwide; of those people, most are asymptomatic [7]. In 2020, there were an estimated 374 million new infections of four curable STIs (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and trichomoniasis) among individuals aged 15 to 49 years [7]. In 2022, an estimated eight million adults in this age group were infected with syphilis [7]. Furthermore, in the same year, approximately 1.1 million pregnant women were estimated to have been infected with syphilis, resulting in over 390,000 adverse birth outcomes [7], and indicating a direct impact on sexual and reproductive health.

sec.title: Syphilis
sec.p: Syphilis is caused by the bacteria Treponema pallidum, and clinical features of the infection vary by disease stage. The WHO estimated 19.9 million prevalent and 6.3 million new cases among individuals aged 15 to 49 years in 2016 [29], increasing to 24.5 million by 2022 [30]. Congenital syphilis, resulting from vertical transmission (typically from mother to child), can cause stillbirth, prematurity, and other complications. It affects an estimated one million pregnancies annually worldwide [11, 12].

sec.title: Gonorrhoea
sec.p: Gonorrhoea is caused by the bacteria Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It is a major cause of urethritis (inflammation of the urethra) in men and cervicitis (inflammation of the lower, narrow end of the uterus (cervix)) in women, potentially leading to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain. The WHO estimated 82.4 million cases globally in 2020 [31], with the highest incidence in the African region, followed by Southeast Asia [31]. Extragenital infections (pharyngeal (throat), rectal) are common among MSM. Although rare, invasive infections such as disseminated gonococcal infection, endocarditis, and meningitis can cause severe morbidity [32, 33, 34].

sec.title: Chlamydia
sec.p: Chlamydia trachomatis is a major cause of PID and represents a significant public health burden due to its strong association with impaired female fertility [35]. PID requires longer and more intensive treatment than urethritis, and it can also spread to the fallopian tubes, cause tubal blockage and adhesions, and ultimately lead to infertility [9, 10]. C trachomatis is also the most common STI that causes urethritis. Most of the affected people are asymptomatic and, thus, provide an ongoing reservoir for infection. The WHO estimated that in 2020, there were 129 million new C trachomatis infections among individuals aged 15 to 49 years, with a prevalence of 4% and 2.5% among women and men, respectively [31]. While some people develop urethritis, chlamydia is often asymptomatic. This makes ‘ping‐pong’ transmission between partners a significant concern, as people may unknowingly transmit the infection. This highlights the importance of treating both the patient and their sexual partner when C trachomatis infection is diagnosed. Moreover, both men and women can experience clinical syndromes due to C trachomatis infection at common epithelial sites, including the rectum and conjunctivae.

sec.title: Description of the intervention and how it might work
sec.p: Post‐exposure prophylaxis (PEP) with antibiotics is a method of preventing STIs by taking antibiotics after sexual intercourse. Doxycycline is the most commonly used PEP agent. It is a broad‐spectrum tetracycline‐class antibiotic that is active against T pallidum (syphilis), C trachomatis, and some strains of N gonorrhoeae [4]. Historically, doxycycline has been used successfully for PEP of other types of infections, such as Lyme disease and leptospirosis [36, 37]. Doxycycline is generally safe and well‐tolerated when used for a long time. The standard practice of PEP administration for preventing STIs is 200 mg doxycycline ideally within 24 hours after sexual activity, but no later than 72 hours is usual [22, 23, 24]. Adverse effects of doxycycline include nausea and vomiting in the short‐term, which are often tolerable with time [38].

A prospective study on the efficacy of minocycline in preventing gonorrhoea showed that 200 mg minocycline taken soon after potential exposure following casual sex offered men partial protection against gonorrhoea. However, the study was abandoned due to concerns about the emergence of antimicrobial resistance in Neisseria gonorrhoeae to tetracyclines [39]. In South Africa, an uncontrolled study (lacking a comparison/control group) documented reduced STI prevalence (such as, chlamydia and gonorrhoea infection) after monthly azithromycin periodical antimicrobial treatment [40]. In addition to azithromycin, doxycycline is highly effective for C trachomatis and T pallidum infections. Doxycycline has good tolerability, high bioavailability with oral dosing, a 20‐hour half‐life, and excellent tissue penetration [41].

Doxycycline PEP (Doxy‐PEP) has shown promise in reducing bacterial STIs among MSM and transgender women. Luetkemeyer and colleagues showed that 200 mg doxycycline within 72 hours after unprotected sex (sex without a condom) reduced the incidence of chlamydia, syphilis, and gonorrhoea by approximately two‐thirds, with good tolerability and no significant increase in antimicrobial resistance [22]. Similarly, in the ANRS IPERGAY substudy, Doxy‐PEP reduced the overall risk of first STI by 47%, with significant reductions in chlamydia and syphilis infections, but not gonorrhoea [23]. These findings support Doxy‐PEP as a potential strategy for preventing STIs.

sec.title: Why it is important to do this review
sec.p: Despite current STI screening programmes, STI incidence is increasing. The ‘test and treat’ approach may not contribute to suppressing STIs. Antibiotic PEP could have an impact on preventing STIs [22, 23].

There is ongoing concern about the increase in drug‐resistant bacteria. An increase in N gonorrhoeae resistant to doxycycline, due to the widespread use of Doxy‐PEP, could become a public health problem [42]. Thus, doxycycline is not recommended for gonorrhoea treatment, as resistance to tetracyclines is already common. Further increases in drug resistance are unlikely to affect current treatment, which does not rely on tetracyclines.

Computer modelling studies have shown that Doxy‐PEP can select for cross‐resistance in Neisseria gonorrhoeae to ceftriaxone, which is used to treat people with gonorrhoea [42, 43]. Additional data from a Doxy‐PEP trial, by Luetkemeyer and colleagues [22], reported that the N gonorrhoeae in the Doxy‐PEP arm had acquired resistance to a greater number of other antibiotics (azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, and benzylpenicillin) compared with those in the control arm [44]. In addition, the rate of doxycycline‐resistant Staphylococcus aureus increased from baseline (5%) to month 12 (13%) [44]. Thus, there are concerns that PEP may affect the normal bacterial flora of people by promoting antimicrobial resistance, including the selection of resistance in Neisseria gonorrhoeae to antibiotics used for its treatment, associated with doxycycline use and broader impacts on antimicrobial resistance [45]. There is also concern about the gut microbiome (microbes that live in a person's gut) and resistome (i.e. the collection of all antimicrobial resistance genes (ARGs) in the microbiome). A 2025 study showed that repeated use of Doxy‐PEP correlated with an increase in the proportion of tetracycline ARGs comprising the gut resistome and an increase in the expression of tetracycline ARGs [46].

Notably, C trachomatis has not developed culture‐based tetracycline drug resistance, despite decades of doxycycline use to treat it. So, it could be a candidate agent for PEP after sexual intercourse. However, studies have reported treatment failures of C trachomatis infection following tetracycline therapy, and decreased in vitro susceptibility and multidrug resistance to tetracyclines [47, 48, 49, 50]. Due to the difficulty in culturing syphilis in vitro, there are few studies regarding the drug resistance of syphilis. In addition, Doxy‐PEP may delay the diagnosis of syphilis [51].

The purpose of this Cochrane review is to assess the current evidence base regarding PEP with antibiotics for the incidence of STIs. It aims to help clinicians, policymakers, and others with their evidence‐based decision‐making regarding PEP for people at high risk of STI infection. It may also highlight areas of focus for future research.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To assess the benefits and harms of taking antibiotics after sexual intercourse as post‐exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for the incidence of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) in adolescents and adults.

sec.title: Criteria for considering studies for this review
Types of studies
sec.p: We will incorporate all individual randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and cluster‐RCTs, irrespective of their publication status (published and unpublished articles, conference abstracts, and letters), language, and the country where the study was conducted. We will exclude studies conducted using crossover or quasi‐experimental methods.

sec.title: Study setting
sec.p: We will include studies conducted in any study setting, such as healthcare settings (e.g. STI clinics, HIV treatment centres, general hospitals), community‐based programmes, research studies, and online platforms to ensure comprehensive coverage of the population at risk.

sec.title: Types of participants
sec.p: We will include the following participants.

list-item.p: Adolescents and adults (men and women). STI incidence is high among young adults, including adolescents [52]. For the purpose of this review, we define adolescents and young adults as individuals aged 15 to 29 years, in accordance with the age classifications commonly used in public health research and consistent with our planned subgroup analysis.

Individuals at high risk of acquiring STIs, including MSM [53], people living with HIV [4], individuals without HIV who are taking PrEP for prevention [4], transgender women [54, 55], transgender men [54, 55], commercial sex workers regardless of gender [54], and individuals with a history of STIs [4, 54]. We will not exclude any participants based on their condom use or gender.

sec.p: If a study includes both eligible and ineligible participants, we will include the study if eligible participants constitute most of the participants (≥ 80%).

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Any antibiotics as PEP within 72 hours of sexual intercourse, based on the assumption that the drug used as PEP, which is administered within 72 hours of exposure, may deliver sufficient chemoprophylaxis to abort primary infection. We will define regular oral antibiotics as prophylaxis as an intervention among patients who have frequent sexual intercourse. We will not exclude any interventions due to the type of antibiotic. Antibiotics should be taken orally.

For the purpose of this Cochrane review, we pre‐specify that PEP typically consists of a single oral dose of an antibiotic taken after a sexual exposure. In recent trials, Doxy‐PEP has most commonly been administered as a single 200 mg dose taken as soon as possible (within 24 hours, and no later than 72 hours) after sexual intercourse [22, 23, 24]. In addition to true PEP regimens, we will also include studies that evaluate periodical or regular prophylaxis, where antibiotics are administered independent of individual sexual exposures. Such regimens include, for example, monthly azithromycin 1 g, monthly ciprofloxacin 500 mg, or other scheduled dosing strategies used in trials among sex workers. These will be included provided that the study authors clearly define the dose, frequency, and timing. We will therefore include (1) one‐time antibiotic doses taken after each sexual exposure, and (2) regularly scheduled prophylactic regimens (e.g. daily, weekly, or monthly) intended to reduce STI acquisition among individuals at increased sexual risk.

sec.title: Control
sec.p: No antibiotics after sexual intercourse or have taken an identical placebo after sexual intercourse.
We will include the intervention of interest combined with another intervention (co‐intervention), such as the following.

list-item.p: PrEP against HIV infection with any antiretrovirals
Combined use of contraceptives in women
Providing contingency management
Service of counselling and testing for STIs and HIV
Vaccination to prevent infections such as Neisseria species and human papillomavirus (HPV)

sec.title: Outcome measures
sec.p: We are aware that outcomes considered meaningful, addressed in a systematic review, may not have been reported in the primary studies. We are also aware that this also arises with the timing of the outcome measurement, where time points determined as clinically meaningful in a review are not measured in the primary studies. So, we will include and discuss all critical and important outcomes in the review that will highlight gaps in primary research and encourage researchers to address these gaps in future studies.

list-item.p: The first episode of any bacterial STI (syphilis, gonorrhoea, and chlamydia infection) at the first time of testing for STIs after trial enrolment. For those who had an STI at the time of screening, the next episode was considered the first episode. The testing for STIs includes nucleic acid amplification testing, or culture of samples obtained from the pharynx, rectum, and urine to check for the presence of gonorrhoea and chlamydia, and syphilis serological studies of blood. Any site of infection is acceptable. We will also report co‐infections/multiple infections at multiple sites within the same person.
First episode of syphilis
First episode of gonorrhoea
First episode of chlamydia
All adverse events during the period of follow‐up of the trial, including all adverse events as defined by the trial authors
Specific adverse events: diarrhoea; nausea or vomiting; these are common side effects of antimicrobial agents, and we will analyse each separately and in detail

Antimicrobial resistance reported at follow‐up and baseline for N gonorrhoeae and commensal bacteria if collected, and the methods for determining antimicrobial resistance and the number of isolates relative to participants
Data on drug adherence per sexual intercourse and the method for objectively evaluating adherence

sec.title: Search methods for identification of studies
sec.p: An experienced Information Specialist (librarian, KSa) will develop and assess the electronic search strategies in collaboration with the review team, following the Peer Review of Electronic Search Strategies (PRESS) guideline [56]. Once finalised, the Information Specialist will perform the searches. We aim to identify all relevant RCTs irrespective of publication status or language. If we find papers that require translation, we will use the Cochrane Engage service to identify volunteers who can assist.

sec.title: Electronic searches
sec.p: We will search the following databases.

list-item.p: Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL; latest issue), in the Cochrane Library (which includes the Cochrane Infectious Diseases Group Specialised Register and the Cochrane Sexually Transmitted Infections Group Register)
PubMed via MEDLINE (1946 to present)
Embase via Ovid (1974 to present)

sec.p: The draft search strategies are in Supplementary material 1.

sec.title: Searching other resources
sec.p: We will search the following trial registers for ongoing and unpublished studies.

list-item.p: ClinicalTrials.gov (www.clinicaltrials.gov)
WHO International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP) (apps.who.int/trialsearch/)

sec.p: The draft search strategies are in Supplementary material 1.
We will identify conference abstracts and unpublished data using CENTRAL and the following conference websites or abstract books, if available.

list-item.p: Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA)
European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases (ESCMID)
National AIDS Treatment Advocacy Project
Conference on Retroviruses and Opportunistic Infections (CROI)
International Union against Sexually Transmitted Infections (IUSTI)
International AIDS Society (IAS)

sec.p: We will screen the reference lists of included studies and related systematic reviews for studies not identified by the database searches.

We will check the bibliographies of the included studies and any relevant systematic reviews for further references to relevant studies. We will contact experts/organisations in the field to obtain additional information on relevant studies. If necessary, we will contact the authors of the included studies for data clarification and further information. We will consider the adverse effects described in the included studies only. We will consider unpublished studies or studies published only as abstracts eligible for inclusion if methods and data can be confirmed by the study author(s).

We will assess the trustworthiness of included studies using Cochrane’s Trustworthiness Screening Tool [57]. If any concerns arise, we will take appropriate actions in accordance with Chapter 4 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [56], including considering post‐publication amendments such as expressions of concern, corrigenda, and retractions.

sec.title: Selection of studies
sec.p: Two review authors (KSh, HO) will independently screen the search results by title or abstract, or both, using Rayyan software [58, 59]. Two review authors will assess the full‐text articles of all potentially eligible studies. Subsequently, the review authors will use the full text to determine whether the titles and abstracts can be included in the review independently. If it is an abstract‐only study, and the two review authors are unsure whether it meets the inclusion criteria of the review, we will contact the study author(s). During screening, the two review authors will resolve any disagreements through discussion, or consulting a third review author, if necessary.

sec.title: Data extraction and management
sec.p: Two review authors (KSh, HO) will independently perform data extraction. The two review authors will resolve any disagreements through discussion and, if necessary, by consulting a third review author. We will contact the study author(s) also if needed. For the data extraction form, we will use a form piloted on 10 randomly selected included studies.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment in included studies
sec.p: Two review authors will independently use the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) tool to assess bias in the included studies [60]. The two review authors will resolve any disagreements through discussion, or by consulting a third review author if necessary.

We will evaluate the effect of assignment to the interventions (intention‐to‐treat (ITT) effect) as our primary estimate of interest, and thus will apply the corresponding RoB 2 version as described in Chapter 8 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [61]. We will assess the risk of bias at the outcome level, not at the study level, using the signalling questions under the following domains.

list-item.p: Bias arising from the randomisation process
Bias due to deviations from intended interventions
Bias due to missing outcome data
Bias in measurement of the outcome
Bias in selection of the reported result

sec.p: For cluster‐RCTs, we will also assess bias arising from the identification or recruitment of participants within clusters. We will use the variants of RoB 2 for cluster RCTs if we identify eligible trials with these study designs [60].

For each outcome, we will use the signalling questions to reach one of three judgments: ‘low risk of bias’, ‘some concerns’, or ‘high risk of bias’. We will use the RoB Excel tool to record answers to the signalling questions, and we will make these answers available in an online repository. We will summarise the risk of bias judgements across different studies for each of the domains for each prespecified outcome. The tool will derive an overall judgment for each study as follows.

list-item.p: Low risk of bias: all domains have a ‘low risk’ judgment
Some concerns: there are ‘some concerns’ in at least one domain, but no domains have a ‘high risk’ judgment
High risk of bias: at least one domain has a ‘high risk’ judgment, or there are ‘some concerns’ in multiple domains

sec.p: As RoB 2 is applied at the outcome level, we will perform RoB 2 assessments for the outcomes listed below. Accordingly, we will apply the RoB 2 tool to the following outcomes.

list-item.p: First episode of any bacterial STI
First episode of syphilis
First episode of gonorrhoea
First episode of chlamydia
All adverse events
Specific adverse events: diarrhoea; nausea or vomiting
Antimicrobial resistance outcomes (N gonorrhoeae and commensal bacteria)

sec.p: These outcomes represent the critical and important outcomes for the summary of findings table(s) (see Certainty of the evidence assessment). Other outcomes will not undergo RoB 2 assessment.

sec.title: Measures of treatment effect
sec.p: We will conduct a meta‐analysis using risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the binary variables. In ITT analysis, the number of total assignments will be used as the denominator, and the number of events that occur will be used as the numerator. For continuous data, we will use the mean difference if outcomes are measured similarly between trials. We will use the standardised mean difference to combine trials that measured the same outcome but used different methods. We will perform a random‐effects meta‐analysis.

sec.title: Unit of analysis issues
sec.p: For the integration of the means and standard deviations (SDs) of continuous variables, we will follow the methods specified in Chapter 6 and Chapter 11 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [62, 63].

If we identify studies with multiple treatment groups, we will report all intervention groups in the ‘Characteristics of included studies’ section and include only those that meet the inclusion criteria. If any trials have multiple arms compared against the same control condition and included in the same meta‐analysis, we will either combine relevant intervention groups to create a single pair‐wise comparison or appropriately adjust the analysis to avoid double‐counting of participants [64].

In the case of cluster‐randomised data, we plan to employ the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC), as discussed in Chapter 23 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [64]. If the ICC value is unavailable, we plan to borrow a suitable factor from other trials as an estimate of relative variability.

sec.title: Dealing with missing data
sec.p: We will perform the intention‐to‐treat (ITT) analysis for all dichotomous data as much as possible. If there is a lack of outcomes for continuous data, we will not impute missing data. If only the standard error or P value are reported, we will calculate the SD using the Altman method [65]. If it is still unknown even after contacting the study author(s), we will calculate the SD from CIs and t‐values using the methods described in Chapter 6 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [63]. Alternatively, we will estimate the SD using other established methods for imputing missing SDs, as described in Chapter 6 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [63, 66]. We will test the validity of these methods in a sensitivity analysis. If there are missing data, we will perform the analysis as a modified ITT (mITT), which includes only those participants who have data available.

sec.title: Reporting bias assessment
sec.p: We will assess reporting bias by evaluating whether the outcomes specified in the study protocols or trial registry entries match those reported in the published results. We will also assess the potential for publication bias by examining studies identified through clinical trial registry searches, including trials with no published results.

If a sufficient number of studies (generally ≥ 10 studies) are available for a given outcome, we will construct funnel plots to visually assess small‐study effects and potential publication bias, in accordance with the guidance in Chapter 13 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [67]. We will construct funnel plots for the following outcomes, provided a sufficient number of studies contribute data to the analysis.

list-item.p: First episode of any bacterial STI
First episode of syphilis
First episode of gonorrhoea
First episode of chlamydia
All adverse events
Specific adverse events: diarrhoea; nausea or vomiting
Antimicrobial resistance outcomes (N gonorrhoeae and commensal bacteria)

sec.p: When applicable, we will also perform statistical tests for funnel plot asymmetry. We will incorporate findings into our overall assessment of reporting bias for each outcome.

In addition, we will use searches of clinical trial registry sites (e.g. ClinicalTrials.gov, WHO ICTRP) to identify completed but unpublished studies. We will compare these studies with published trials to assess selective reporting and to evaluate the possibility of unpublished negative results contributing to publication bias. Registry‐identified studies will be included in funnel plot assessments when they contribute data to the meta‐analysis. However, we will not perform funnel plot asymmetry tests when there are 10 or fewer studies or when included studies have very similar sample sizes.

sec.title: Comparisons of interest
sec.p: We anticipate that most included trials will compare an antibiotic prophylaxis regimen (e.g. doxycycline, fluoroquinolones, macrolides) with no prophylaxis or standard of care. Our primary planned comparison is the following.

list-item.p: Any antibiotic prophylaxis versus no prophylaxis/standard care

sec.p: Where sufficient data exist, we will also analyse the following antibiotic classes separately.

list-item.p: Doxycycline prophylaxis versus no prophylaxis
Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis versus no prophylaxis
Macrolide prophylaxis versus no prophylaxis

sec.p: We will analyse these comparisons separately because different antibiotic classes may differ in their effectiveness and resistance profiles.

sec.title: Outcome structure for synthesis
sec.p: For STIs, we will analyse outcomes at the following two levels.

list-item.p: Combined outcome (primary synthesis)
first episode of any bacterial STI at the first post‐enrolment testing
Pathogen‐specific outcomes (secondary syntheses)
first episode of syphilis, gonorrhoea, or chlamydia, analysed separately if data allow

sec.p: We will perform statistical analysis using RevMan [68]. Where data for more than two trials will be available, we will use a random‐effects model, assuming that individual studies estimated different but related treatment effects, due to differences in factors such as study population, treatment received, and follow‐up times. We will not restrict eligibility because of the risk of bias.

We will use a random‐effects model as we expect clinical diversity. The Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML) estimator will be used to estimate between‐trial variance [69]. We will use the Hartung‐Knapp‐Sidik‐Jonkman method to calculate a CI for the meta‐analysis effect estimate when there are at least three studies, and the estimate of heterogeneity is greater than zero. In other scenarios (i.e. in pooled analyses of two studies, or where the estimate of heterogeneity equals zero), we will use the Wald‐type method.

The random‐effects model summary results provide an estimate of the average treatment effect, and the CIs indicate the uncertainty around these estimates. Results should be interpreted as an estimate of the average effect rather than a single common effect (which is estimated by fixed‐effect models).

Where meta‐analysis is not possible, we will present results in a narrative synthesis and create appropriate tables and figures, following the Synthesis Without Meta‐analysis (SWiM) reporting guideline [70, 71].

sec.title: Investigation of heterogeneity and subgroup analysis
Evaluation of heterogeneity
sec.p: We will describe the clinical diversity and methodological variability of the evidence narratively and in tables. In the tables we will include data on study characteristics, such as design features, population characteristics, and intervention details.

We will visually inspect forest plots to assess statistical heterogeneity and describe the direction and magnitude of effects and the degree of overlap between CIs. We will also consider the statistics generated in forest plots that measure statistical heterogeneity. We will use the I² statistic to quantify inconsistencies between the trials in each analysis. We will also consider the P value from the Chi² test to assess if this heterogeneity is significant (P < 0.1). If we identify substantial heterogeneity, we will report the finding and explore possible explanatory factors, using prespecified subgroup and sensitivity analysis.
We will grade the degree of heterogeneity as follows, following the guidance in Chapter 10 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [69].

list-item.p: 0% to 40% might not represent important heterogeneity
30% to 60% may represent moderate heterogeneity
50% to 90% may represent substantial heterogeneity
More than 75% may represent considerable heterogeneity

sec.p: We will use a rough guideline to interpret the I2 value rather than a simple threshold, and our interpretation will take into account the understanding that measures of heterogeneity (I2 and τ²) will be estimated with high uncertainty when the number of studies is small [69].

sec.title: Subgroup analysis
sec.p: We will conduct subgroup analyses to assess the heterogeneity of clinical study participants and interventions. All subgroup analyses will be conducted at the study level (i.e. between‐study comparisons), not within individual trials. We will not extract or analyse individual participant–level subgroup data.

We will group studies according to the predominant characteristics of their populations as reported by the study authors. If a study includes participants across more than one subgroup category, we will classify the study according to the category that represents most of its participants (e.g. ≥ 80%). If no subgroup represents 80% or more of the sample, we will include the study in a ‘mixed/unspecified’ category for that subgroup analysis.
These subgroup analyses are exploratory and aim to examine whether study‐level characteristics may explain heterogeneity in effect estimates.
We plan to explore the following study‐level subgroups.

list-item.p: Antibiotic class: divided based on the types of antibiotics used. Doxycycline is the most commonly used agent in recent studies [22, 23, 24], but fluoroquinolone and macrolide antibiotics are also used to treat STIs [4]. Therefore, we will perform stratification by type of antibiotic. For example, fluoroquinolones can be an alternative treatment for C trachomatis infection, and fluoroquinolone is one of the treatments for Mycoplasma genitalium, which causes urethritis [4]. Macrolide antibiotics are a treatment for C trachomatis. Although there are concerns about resistance [72], they are also an alternative treatment choice for N gonorrhoeae [4].

Age: divided by age, by dividing it into three age groups: adolescents and young adults aged 15 to 29 years, adults aged 30 to 49 years, and adults aged 50 years and over. Surveillance data show that the incidence of STIs is high among adolescents and young adults aged from 15 to 29 years [52]. The 49 years is based on the upper range for reproductive age in women, and the incidence of STIs decreases after this age range [52].

Gender: divided by gender, comparing cis‐men, transgender women, transgender men, and cis‐women. Differences in the incidence and testing rates of STIs due to gender differences have been noted [54, 55], and thus we have included these in the subgroup analysis.

HIV status: divided by HIV status, comparing people living with HIV to those without HIV. As it has been noted that the incidence and testing rates of STIs differ depending on whether a person is living with HIV or not [4, 55, 73], we will include HIV status in the subgroup analysis.

PrEP (to prevent HIV) status: divided by using PrEP or not. There has been a noted increase in the incidence of STIs among people who use PrEP [15, 74, 75]. For PrEP status, we will conduct subgroup analyses if a sufficient number of studies meet the inclusion criteria.

sec.p: We will formally assess subgroup differences using the Test for Subgroup Differences in RevMan [68], as recommended in Chapter 10 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [69].

sec.title: Equity‐related assessment
sec.p: We will conduct an equity‐related assessment to ensure the intervention's effectiveness and accessibility across different populations [76]. Our assessment will consider several key factors.

First, the review will evaluate whether socioeconomic status, including education level [77], influences the intervention's outcomes (Supplementary material 2). A low level of education has been reported as being related to a higher risk of acquiring STIs. For example, a low level of education is linked to a low level of awareness of one's own HIV risk due to a lack of interest in PrEP to prevent HIV [78], a risk factor for pregnancy syphilis [79], and less communication with one's partner regarding the treatment of STIs [80]. In addition, we will consider geographic location, and will compare low‐ and middle‐income countries/regions (such as Africa, Asia, and Latin America) and high‐income countries/regions (such as North America and Europe) to identify any regional disparities [76].

We will examine the impact of the intervention on different ethnic and cultural backgrounds to ensure inclusivity. Gender and sexual orientation will be analysed, comparing results across men, women, transgender individuals, and various sexual orientations [76]. It has been reported that nationality and gender are predictors of whether someone has knowledge of STIs [81]. Stigma regarding sexual behaviour may limit disclosure of sexual orientation and practices, thus limiting access to appropriate HIV prevention services [82]. We will examine these characteristics to understand their potential role in modifying intervention effects.

In addition, we will assess the effect of age differences, particularly between younger/adolescents, middle age, and older adults. We will also consider the effects on individuals living with HIV compared to those without HIV, to assess whether the intervention effects differ between these groups.

sec.title: Identification of equity‐related evidence
sec.p: During title/abstract screening and full‐text review, we will systematically identify studies that report information relevant to the PROGRESS‐Plus framework (place of residence, race/ethnicity, occupation, gender identity, religion, education, socioeconomic status, social capital, plus age, disability, and sexual orientation) [83]. We will record equity‐relevant information when it appears in baseline characteristics, eligibility criteria, subgroup descriptions, or contextual descriptions of the study population or setting [84].

sec.title: Data extraction for equity
sec.p: We will extract equity‐related variables using a predefined data extraction form [84]. Two review authors will independently extract these data. These extracted variables will include (where reported) the following.

list-item.p: Demographic composition (age distribution, gender identity, sexual orientation)
HIV status and PrEP use
Socioeconomic indicators (education, income level, employment status)
Geographic or healthcare setting (urban/rural, country income level)
Representation of key populations (e.g. MSM, sex workers, adolescents, people living with HIV)
Access to STI prevention or testing services
Contextual barriers or facilitators to care
Subgroup‐specific outcome data, if reported

sec.p: Where reported, we will also extract contextual factors, such as experiences of sexual abuse, stigma, or availability of support services, as these fall within the ‘Plus’ elements of the PROGRESS‐Plus framework and may influence access to STI prevention interventions. We will incorporate these data into the ‘Characteristics of included studies’ tables.

sec.title: Appraisal of equity considerations
sec.p: We will appraise the extent to which each study reports equity‐related characteristics and whether these factors may influence intervention effects [84]. Specifically, we will assess the following.

list-item.p: Completeness and quality of reporting of PROGRESS‐Plus variables
Inclusion or exclusion of populations at differential risk of STIs
Contextual factors (e.g. stigma, service access, socioeconomic conditions) that may modify treatment effects
Availability and usefulness of subgroup‐specific effect estimates

sec.p: We will report the findings from this appraisal descriptively in the Results section. This will inform our interpretation of the applicability, external validity, and generalisability of the review's findings [85].

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: We will conduct sensitivity analyses for the primary outcomes to determine whether the conclusions to arbitrary decisions made regarding the eligibility and analysis will be robust. We will perform sensitivity analyses by excluding trials of lower quality based on a lack of the following: adequate randomisation, allocation concealment, and less than 10% loss to follow‐up.

sec.title: Certainty of the evidence assessment
sec.p: We will create summary of findings table(s) to summarise our findings for the comparison of post‐exposure antibiotic prophylaxis versus no treatment/placebo, following guidance in Chapter 14 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [86]. To ensure the summary of findings table(s) contains no more than the recommended seven rows, we will prioritise the most clinically informative and widely reported outcomes. We will include the following outcomes in the tables.

list-item.p: First episode of any bacterial STI
First episode of syphilis
First episode of gonorrhoea
First episode of chlamydia
All adverse events, as defined by the trial authors
Specific adverse events: diarrhoea; nausea or vomiting
Antimicrobial resistance outcomes (N gonorrhoeae and commensal bacteria)

sec.p: Two review authors (KSh, HO) will independently assess the certainty of the evidence according to the five GRADE domains (study limitations, consistency of effect, imprecision, indirectness, and publication bias) using GRADEpro GDT software [87]. They will follow the guidance in Chapter 14 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [86]. The two review authors will resolve any disagreements by discussion, or by consulting a third review author.
Each table contains a grading of evidence based on the GRADE approach [88]. The GRADE approach results in an assessment of the certainty of a body of evidence in one of the following four grades.

list-item.p: High: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect.
Moderate: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate; the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited; the true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect.
Very low: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate; the true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect.

sec.p: We will report results in accordance with Cochrane MECIR standards [85]. We will report results in accordance with recommended narrative statements, as described in Chapter 15 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [89].
We will justify all decisions to downgrade the certainty of the evidence using footnotes. We will make comments to aid the reader's understanding of the review where necessary.

sec.title: Consumer involvement
sec.p: Engaging consumers in this process enhances research quality, promotes transparency, and fosters trust between researchers and the community. At the review stage, consumers will assist with the interpretation/explanation of the findings to a lay audience, thus adding valuable context and highlighting practical implications. The involvement of consumers will ensure the results are accessible and applicable to the target population. We will acknowledge consumers who provide their insights in the Acknowledgements section of the published Cochrane review. We will offer the option to contribute anonymously, given the sensitive nature of the topic and the possibility that some consumers may not wish to be identified. We will only name individuals if they explicitly consent to be named.

We will involve a patient who is an MSM living with HIV at the review stage. By involving this consumer at the review stage, we hope to bridge the gap between consumer knowledge and evidence by listening to perspectives rooted in real‐life clinical settings, and make the review more inclusive for a wider range of people by identifying potentially discriminatory expressions or statements. We believe these processes will help ensure that the review is aligned with the needs of the communities who are most affected.

sec.title: Supporting Information
sec.p: Supplementary materials are available with the online version of this article: 10.1002/14651858.CD016125.

Supplementary materials are published alongside the article and contain additional data and information that support or enhance the article. Supplementary materials may not be subject to the same editorial scrutiny as the content of the article and Cochrane has not copyedited, typeset or proofread these materials. The material in these sections has been supplied by the author(s) for publication under a Licence for Publication and the author(s) are solely responsible for the material. Cochrane accordingly gives no representations or warranties of any kind in relation to, and accepts no liability for any reliance on or use of, such material.

caption.p: Supplementary material 2 Summary of the characteristics of participants we would expect to see

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: Cochrane Sexually Transmitted Infections supported the authors in the development of this protocol. Elena Kostova (Amsterdam UMC, University of Amsterdam) provided advice and guidance on the Background section.

sec.title: Editorial and peer‐reviewer contributions
sec.p: The following people conducted the editorial process for this protocol:

list-item.p: Sign‐off Editors (editorial decisions): Toby Lasserson, Cochrane; and Jillian T Henderson, Kaiser Permanente Center for Health Research Evidence‐based Practice Center; Oregon Health & Science University Fertility Regulation Cochrane Group;
Managing Editors (selected peer reviewers, provided editorial guidance to authors, edited the article): Liz Bickerdike, Jessica Thomas, and Joey Kwong, Cochrane Central Editorial Service;
Editorial Assistant (conducted editorial policy checks, collated peer‐reviewer comments and supported the editorial team): Jacob Hester, Cochrane Central Editorial Service;
Copy Editor (copy editing and production): Deirdre Walshe, Cochrane Central Production Service;

Peer reviewers (provided comments and recommended an editorial decision): Fabian Kong, The University of Melbourne (clinical/content review); Professor Doctor Jason Ong, Monash University, Australia (clinical/content review); Anbuselvi Danapalan, Infection Prevention & Control Practitioner, Al Ahli Hospital, Qatar and Honorary Lecturer, Queen Margaret University, UK (consumer review); Jo‐Ana Chase, Cochrane Evidence Production and Methods Directorate (methods review); Jo Platt, Central Editorial Information Specialist (search review).

sec.title: Disclosure of artificial intelligence (AI) use
sec.p: We used ChatGPT(OpenAI; version GPT‐5.1) only for proofreading assistance (e.g. typographical checks). No AI tools were used for content generation, data extraction, analysis, or methodological decisions.

sec.title: Contributions of authors
sec.p: KSh developed the research question, designed the review, drafted the protocol, and lead the overall methodology. KSh developed and refined the search strategy with support from the Information Specialist (KSa).
KSa developed the draft electronic search strategies in collaboration with the review team, following the Peer Review of Electronic Search Strategies (PRESS) guideline.

HO and KI contributed to the development of the eligibility criteria and data extraction procedures, and reviewed all protocol drafts. HO and KI also contributed to planning the analysis and interpretation strategies.
EO provided methodological and content expertise, assisted in drafting the protocol, and commented on all drafts.
NM provided clinical and methodological expertise, assisted in writing the protocol, and commented on all drafts.
All authors read and approved the final version of the protocol.

sec.title: Declarations of interest
sec.p: KSh has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.
HO has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.
KSa has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.
EO has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.
KI has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.
NM has no commercial or non‐commercial conflicts of interest relevant to this review.

list-item.p: None, Other
We have no internal source of support
None, Other
We have no external source of support

sec.title: Registration and protocol
sec.p: Cochrane approved the proposal for this review in February 2024.

sec.title: Data, code and other materials
sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as it is a protocol, so no datasets were generated or analyzed.
